foods.gut_notes: Kombucha contains live cultures (typically Lactobacillus, Acetobacter, Saccharomyces) and organic acids (acetic, gluconic) which lower colonic pH favouring beneficial bacteria. Polyphenols from tea provide mild anti-inflammatory benefit. Small 120ml portion limits probiotic dose—typical therapeutic dose is 200-250ml. No flagged foods present. Consider increasing to full glass (240ml) and ensure plain yoghurt 150g daily per doctor targets for stronger probiotic support.
foods.name: Kombucha (fermented tea beverage)

meal_description: 100g of oil roasted peas (mutter)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch and fibre from peas are fermented to butyrate, primary fuel for this keystone species
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fibre fermentation produces short-chain fatty acids that support butyrate-producing species
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin and small amounts of inulin provide modest bifidogenic substrate
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols in peas (though reduced by roasting) support mucus-layer bacteria indirectly
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Oil roasting increases fat content; excess dietary fat promotes bile-tolerant Bilophila (minor concern at 100g portion)
bacteria_harmed.name: Bilophila wadsworthia

foods.gut_notes: Green peas are an excellent legume source of prebiotic fibre and resistant starch. 100g is a moderate, FODMAP-safe portion. Roasting method preserves most fibre but may reduce heat-sensitive polyphenols compared to steaming. The added oil increases fat content—while peas themselves are anti-inflammatory (polyphenols, vitamin K), excessive oil could theoretically promote Bilophila wadsworthia, though this portion size is unlikely to cause concern. Does NOT meet doctor targets (boiled chickpeas 80g 3x/week preferred for legume intake). Consider alternating with steamed peas or boiled chickpeas for optimal gut benefit. No flagged foods present.
foods.name: Green peas (mutter), oil roasted

meal_description: 2 roti with egg bhurji, raw onion slice, and vinegar pickled beetroot slice

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch from cooked wheat is fermented to butyrate, primary fuel for this species
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Butyrate-producing bacteria benefits from resistant starch fermentation
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

foods.gut_notes: Cooked wheat roti is LOW FODMAP at this portion. Provides resistant starch which is excellent for patient's deficient Faecalibacterium prausnitzii. However, 2 rotis (120g) exceeds doctor's target of 1 slice bread equivalent; consider reducing to 1 roti. Wheat contains gluten—monitor if patient has sensitivity.
foods.name: Roti (wheat flatbread)

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High dietary fat from fried eggs promotes secondary bile acid metabolism, favoring this pathogenic species
bacteria_harmed.name: Bilophila wadsworthia

foods.gut_notes: Eggs are protein-rich and contain choline (supports gut barrier), but frying method increases oxidized lipids and inflammatory compounds. Fried preparation elevates risk of promoting Bilophila wadsworthia (patient has excess). Boiling or poaching would be preferable. No prebiotic value. Neutral to mildly inflammatory depending on oil used.
foods.name: Egg bhurji (scrambled/fried eggs)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Raw onion is rich in inulin and FOS, which selectively feed this BOOST species
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols and prebiotic fibres indirectly support mucus-layer colonization
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High FODMAP content may ferment rapidly, producing gas and favoring this pathogenic species in excess
bacteria_harmed.name: Ruminococcus gnavus

foods.gut_notes: ⚠️ CRITICAL FLAG: Raw onion at ~35g EXCEEDS FODMAP threshold (safe limit <2 tbsp cooked ≈15g raw). This portion is HIGH FODMAP and will likely trigger bloating, gas, and IBS symptoms in sensitive patients. While inulin/FOS are excellent prebiotics for Bifidobacterium longum (patient's BOOST target), the raw form and excessive portion make this problematic. RECOMMENDATION: Omit raw onion or reduce to <10g (thin slice only), or cook onion to reduce FODMAP load by ~50%.
foods.name: Raw onion slice

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols (betalains) and pectin support mucus-layer health and this species' growth
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: Excellent addition. Pickled beetroot is LOW FODMAP at this portion. Rich in betalains (anti-inflammatory polyphenols) and pectin. Vinegar adds acetic acid which supports butyrate production. Does NOT contain live probiotics (vinegar is not fermented with live cultures, just preserved in vinegar). Strongly anti-inflammatory. Supports patient's deficient Akkermansia muciniphila. One of the best components of this meal.
foods.name: Vinegar pickled beetroot slice
